text: Three closed ports are visible on the right chest, with CO₂ insufflation used to expand the surgical field. The surgeon begins dissection medial to the right phrenic nerve, incising the mediastinal pleura. The dissection progresses superiorly along the superior vena cava, with pulsating mammary vessels visible on the left side of the screen.

The surgeon continues dissection underneath the sternum, entering and widely opening the left pleural space. Thymic tissue is dissected from the ascending aorta and innominate vein. A 5mm Trocar is inserted into the left chest to enhance visualization and facilitate dissection along the left phrenic nerve, with the dissection proceeding between the pericardium and the inferior portion of the thymus gland.

The surgeon grasps and retracts the thymus towards the right, facilitating dissection around the aorta and pericardium. Thymic tissue on the left side has been excised, leaving only the superior poles. The gland is retracted inferiorly and to the right, and dissection of the superior poles is initiated.

The surgeon uses electrocautery to take down ligamentous attachments, skeletonizing the innominate vein. The superior pole dissection extends into the neck, with the thymus retracted to the right. As the left superior pole is dissected, it is retracted to the left to facilitate dissection of the right superior pole. The tissue between the innominate vein and artery is completely dissected, allowing clear visualization of the innominate artery.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the superior pole of the thymus gland, which is then fully excised and placed into an endobag for removal from the chest cavity. A post-operative chest X-ray is displayed, and a sixteen French chest tube is visible, which was placed at the end of the surgery and later removed in the PACU.
